title: The 99 Names of Allah (Asma ul Husna): Arabic, Meanings, Audio and Transliteration
excerpt: The 99 Names of Allah are not a list to memorise — they are 99 doors into knowing the Creator of the universe personally. Explore every Name with its Arabic text, correct transliteration, deep meaning, and audio pronunciation.
author: QuranBookk
content:
## Knowing About Allah vs. Truly Knowing Allah

There is a difference between knowing *about* someone and truly *knowing* them. You can know about a person — their job title, their physical description, facts about their history — without knowing them. Truly knowing someone requires knowing their character: how they think, how they respond, what they value, how they relate to the world.

The 99 Names of Allah — *Asma ul Husna* (الأسماء الحسنى), the Most Beautiful Names — are not a list of labels for Allah. They are *who Allah is*. Each Name is a window into the character of the Creator: how He relates to creation, how He responds to sin, how He holds power, how He loves, how He provides, how He sees.

The Prophet ﷺ said:

<HadithBlock
  text="Allah has ninety-nine names, one hundred minus one. Whoever encompasses them (ahsaha) will enter Paradise."
  narrator="Abu Hurairah (رضي الله عنه)"
  source="Sahih al-Bukhari 2736, Sahih Muslim 2677"
/>

The word *ahsaha* is critical. It does not mean *memorises* — it means *encompasses*, *comprehends*, *lives by*, *supplicates through*. A Muslim who has memorised all 99 Names phonetically but knows nothing of their meaning has not *ahsaha* them. A Muslim who deeply knows ten Names — understanding, believing in, acting upon, and supplicating through them — is closer to the hadith's meaning.

The [QuranBookk 99 Names of Allah feature](/99-names-of-allah) provides every Name with Arabic text, correct transliteration, audio pronunciation, and full meaning — the tools for genuine *ahsaha*.

## What the Feature Provides

For each of the 99 Names:
- **Arabic text** — the Name in the standard Arabic script
- **Transliteration** — phonetic rendering for correct pronunciation
- **Audio** — hear the Name pronounced correctly
- **Meaning** — the full English meaning, including the root word and its semantic dimensions
- **Quranic context** — where and how the Name appears in the Quran

## The Most Essential Names to Know — With Their Deep Meanings

### The Names of Mercy

**Ar-Rahman (الرَّحْمَن) — The Entirely Merciful**
Ar-Rahman describes the mercy that encompasses all of creation — every human being, every animal, every living thing. The rain that falls on the fields of a disbeliever falls from Ar-Rahman. The food that nourishes a sinner comes from Ar-Rahman. This mercy is universal, all-encompassing, not contingent on faith or obedience. It is so fundamental that Allah begins the Quran — after Al-Fatihah — with *Ar-Rahman 'allamal Quran*: *"The Entirely Merciful has taught the Quran."*

**Ar-Raheem (الرَّحِيم) — The Especially Merciful**
Where Ar-Rahman is the vast mercy of existence itself, Ar-Raheem is the intimate, personal mercy Allah has specifically for the believers. Both names derive from *rahma* (mercy), but Ar-Raheem carries the *fa'eel* form — a continuous, active mercy that keeps returning, keeps providing, keeps caring.

**Al-Ghaffar (الغَفَّار) — The Repeatedly Forgiving**
Not *Al-Ghafir* (the Forgiver, once) but *Al-Ghaffar* — the intensive, repetitive form. Al-Ghaffar forgives the same sin from the same person a hundred times, if that person returns in sincere tawbah a hundred times. The Name is a direct theological rebuke to despair: no one has sinned too many times for Al-Ghaffar.

**Al-Tawwab (التَّوَّاب) — The Ever-Accepting of Repentance**
The root *tawbah* means to turn. Al-Tawwab not only accepts when the servant turns toward Allah — He *turns toward* the servant who repents. The Name describes a mutual turning: the servant turns in repentance; Allah turns in mercy and forgiveness.

### The Names of Power

**Al-Qadir / Al-Qadeer (القَادِر / القَدِير) — The All-Powerful**
Nothing is outside Allah's ability. Not the healing of a terminal illness. Not the reversal of an impossible situation. Not the opening of a door that has been closed for years. *Al-Qadir* is the Name to invoke when you are standing before what appears to be an impossibility.

**Al-Fattah (الفَتَّاح) — The Opener, The Judge**
Al-Fattah opens what is closed and judges between what is contested. When a door has been shut — a job, a relationship, an opportunity, a path forward — Al-Fattah is the Name of invocation. *"Fa inna ma'al-'usri yusra"* — *"Indeed, with hardship comes ease"* (94:5) — Al-Fattah is the Name behind that promise.

**Al-Jabbar (الجَبَّار) — The Compeller, The Restorer**
The Arabic root *jabr* — from which the English word *algebra* derives — means to set broken bones back into place. Al-Jabbar both compels creation to His will and *restores what is shattered*. For a Muslim whose life has broken — in grief, in loss, in failure — Al-Jabbar is the Name of the One who mends.

### The Names of Knowledge

**Al-Aleem (العَلِيم) — The All-Knowing**
Every thought you have never said aloud. Every intention behind every action. Every moment of your life that no one witnessed. Every hadith clarifies: *"Allah knows what the hearts conceal."* Al-Aleem knows it all — and continues to sustain and provide for the one whose thoughts He fully knows.

**Al-Khabeer (الخَبِير) — The Fully Aware**
Beyond knowing — Al-Khabeer knows the hidden internal structures of all things. Not just what you did, but why. Not just the surface, but the depths. The Name comes from *khabara* — to probe, to investigate thoroughly, to know intimately.

**Al-Shaheed (الشَّهِيد) — The Witness**
Allah witnesses every moment of your life — including the moments you believe are unseen. This Name transforms loneliness: you are never unwitnessed, even in your most private moments of worship or struggle.

### The Names of Beauty

**Al-Wadud (الوَدُود) — The Loving, The Affectionate**
Not merely *loving* but *Al-Wadud* — from *wudd*, an Arabic word for a tender, affectionate love that is caring and warm. This is the Name that describes Allah's specific, personal love for the believers — not general goodwill, but something closer to what the word *caring* approaches in English.

**Al-Latif (اللَّطِيف) — The Subtle, The Gentle**
Al-Latif operates in ways imperceptible to the human eye. The job opportunity that came through an unexpected conversation. The delay that prevented you from being somewhere at the wrong moment. The connection that formed at what seemed like the wrong time. Al-Latif is the Name for Allah's hidden, gentle orchestration of circumstances.

## Supplicating Through the Names

The Names are not just for learning — they are for invoking. The Quran instructs:

<AyahBlock
  arabic="وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ فَٱدْعُوهُ بِهَا"
  translation="And to Allah belong the Most Beautiful Names, so call upon Him by them."
  reference="Surah Al-A'raf 7:180"
/>

Matching the Name to the request is the practice: ask for provision through Al-Razzaq. Ask for forgiveness through Al-Ghaffar. Ask for healing through Al-Shafi. Ask for a difficult door to open through Al-Fattah. Ask for mercy on the Day of Judgement through Ar-Raheem.

For building a dua practice around the Names, the [Quranic Duas collection](/quran/ayats/) shows how the Prophets themselves invoked Allah's Names in their supplications. The [Digital Tasbih](/digital-tasbih/) can be used to count repetitions of any Name as a Dhikr practice.

## One Name Per Day: A Practical Approach to *Ahsaha*

<Callout type="tip">
Rather than memorising all 99 Names quickly, choose one Name per day. Read its Arabic text, listen to its audio pronunciation, reflect on its meaning for five minutes, and recite it in your Dhikr and dua throughout the day. At the end of 99 days, you will have genuinely known each Name — not memorised a list, but encountered a character.
</Callout>
